I want you to consciously write it down and apply these tonalities to specific spots and practice them. I want you to really practice your tonality and make sure that especially those first few seconds, sharpest attack, enthusiastic, expert in your field, and remember one key point that I brought out today is that while tonality will get you through those first seconds, tonality by language, remember the words matter too. You can't be saying stupid shit. So the idea is that those first few seconds, they capture the person's first impression. Either you're perceived the right way or you're not. But once those first few seconds pass, you're still being judged. So the idea is to wrap these three things together. Ready by language and your words into one seamless package. So write out your patterns here. I want you to write out your patterns. Make sure that no matter how well you know them, I want you to write them out now, today at some point today, and then make some marks on them, the key points where you're applying your tonalities. Don't just take it to the ground. You can always take this to the next level. So mark down, say, this is where I lower my voice. This is where I apply scarcity. This is where I use up tones. Whatever key you use, and I've seen a lot of people on the networking group publish their own little keys. They're all good. Whatever works for you. And I want you to take this tonality today. Just make this the way you take your tonality to the next level, where you lower your voice. Just practice this. You own these tonalities. Practice speaking certain. And remember, the best example of certainty, I think, is when in the movie, The Wolf of the Wall Street, when Leo is talking, he's saying, name of the company, Arrow Time International. It's a cunning edge, and he's keeping his voice low. But the certainty is powering through. Certainty is not yelling. The mistake people make with certainty is they confuse certainty with enthusiasm. The tonality of unbridled enthusiasm. They confuse the answer. It's like, oh my God, it's so great. It's the best. That's not certainty. Certainty is when you have, it's like your solar plexus. It's right here. It's a power that you speak with. Reason for the call. Something, name of the company, Arrow Time, it's better what the words are. The point is it's not yelling and screaming. It's actually lowering your voice, but putting power behind the words. That's the tonality of certainty. You don't sound certain by being just enthusiastic. That's very different. So there's two types of enthusiasm. You have your unbridled, over-the-top enthusiasm, which I don't really believe works at all in sales. It's very rare moments. But what really works well is your bottled enthusiasm. And when you take bottled enthusiasm and then apply a bit of a whisper to it, that power whisper, that's certainty. You're saying, John, this is the reason for the call. This is the best thing I've seen. It's an energy flow. That kind of reason I keep stopping, because the words don't matter. The words have to be read, but it could be at any spot in the sale. It's this power that comes out of you. It's a belief. Remember this. At the highest level, sales is the transference of emotion. And the emotion that you're transferring is what? Certainty. Certainty that your product is awesome. Certainty that you're awesome. Certainty that the company that stands behind you, right? The three tens. The straight line does not create certainty. It transfers certainty. The certainty has to come from you. The analogy I always use is like the boiler in the house, the furnace. The furnace creates heat, and then the straight one, and then the duct work, or the HVAC system, is what you use to transfer heat. So with the furnace doesn't work, I don't care how elegant the duct work is, there's nothing to transfer. The same thing goes in selling with the straight line. I don't care how well you know the straight line system. If you are not yourself in a state of certainty, you have nothing to transfer to the prospect. You get it? The straight line transfers certainty, but you create certainty. It's through your tonality, that lowering your voice at power, that you're just so sure. And as I said in the beginning, we all possess the ability to sound certain because you've done it before. Go back to a moment in your life when you are feeling absolutely certain and recapture that's your tonality. Once you learn to apply tonalities on purpose, guys, it takes the straight line to an entirely different level. Tonality is the hidden language of influence. It allows you to get your point across in a way that otherwise would be offensive. It would not make sense. And by using tonality strategically, and also remember at the higher level, it takes control of the prospect's dialogue, in a monologue. They are literally so busy trying to make sense of the extra words they're hearing that they lose the ability to narrate against you and allow you to control the conversation. So again, I want you to make this, please spend the time. I just realized today that it's like the power of tonality. It's really unmatched in your ability to be perceived the right way, to transfer emotion the right way, and also essentially to harness the power of the straight line. Things like looping, prospect, all those other techniques, they're crucial. They work magically, but it's your tonality that allows you to essentially transfer certainty to some other person, to some other human being. And that's what sales is all about. So don't master all the other aspects, but just ignore tonality. Make sure that you write down your language plans, put your tonal marks in it, and just go practice. Make yourself proficient. Don't settle for second best here. You're capable of greatness. And sometimes for those, some of you love tonality, right? You love practicing. Others just, yeah, I don't like doing that. It seems weird. Remember success is about getting yourself to do the things that you know you have to do even when you don't feel like doing them. That's what it takes to be successful. So whether you like practicing tonality, please make it a point today. Spend some time going through your patterns, writing down which tonality you need to muster and you take yourself, or if someone listens to you, break up into a group with someone else in the network. But don't settle for second best in this department. I promise you, watch what happens. Pick up your tonality, take it to the next level, and I promise you, every other aspect of the straight line will get easier.
